original_abstract:
This study evaluates the influence of microwave power levels during vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) on the drying kinetics and quality attributes of sour cherries. Specifically, we examined temperature profiles alongside critical indicators such as phenolic content, antioxidant capacity, and color degradation. For comparison, samples were also processed using convective drying (CD) at 50, 60, and 70 °C, and freeze-drying (FD) served as the control. To prevent thermal damage at the critical moisture threshold (approx. 1 kg/kg dry mass), the VMD process employed initial power levels of 240, 360, and 480 W, which were incrementally reduced to 240 or 120 W. 

Results indicate that elevated air temperatures in CD and rising material temperatures in VMD both negatively impacted phenolic retention, antioxidant activity, and color stability—changes that correlated closely with anthocyanin degradation. Nevertheless, VMD significantly outperformed CD and proved to be a viable, high-efficiency alternative to FD. The optimal product quality and most vibrant color were achieved using a VMD protocol starting at 480 W and finishing at 120 W. Notably, the drying process appeared to enhance the concentration of quercetin and kaempferol derivatives. These findings suggest that VMD-processed sour cherries maintain high nutritional value and a stable shelf life, offering a high-quality alternative to fresh fruit throughout the year.
humanized_abstract:
The purpose of this research was to investigate the effects of different microwave power levels during the vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) process on drying rates of sour cherries and their quality parameters such as temperature profiles, losses of phenolic compounds, antioxidant activities and color changes. In order to make comparison with VMD process, the samples were dried by using convective drying (CD) at 50, 60 and 70 °C. Freeze-drying (FD) was also used as reference drying method. For maintaining the material below the critical thermal threshold (around 1 kg/kg) without any thermal deteriorations during VMD process, the initial microwave powers of 240, 360 and 480 W were applied which were then reduced to the final powers of 240 or 120 W.

All the quality parameters of extracts obtained from thermally treated CDs were negatively affected as compared to the fresh CDs. Such negative effects included reduction in phenolic content, antiradical activity and colour stability, which all coincided with the loss of anthocyanic pigments. VMD, however, outclassed CD and thereby proved to be a more efficient technique than CD and almost comparable to FD. The optimum VMD parameters were 480 W as starting power and 120 W as ending power. The increased content of derivative quercetin and kaempferol compounds suggest that the drying process of VMD retains the shelf life and nutritional value of its resultant product (sour cherry powder) and can thereby be used for all-year-round consumption of fruits.

document.input:
This study evaluates the influence of microwave power levels during vacuum–microwave drying (VMD) on the drying kinetics and quality attributes of sour cherries.
